title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill gives special gold medals from Congress to the 1980 US Olympic men's ice hockey team. The team won a famous game against the Soviet Union that people call the "Miracle on Ice." Congress wants to honor them because their victory was so important and exciting for America. The medals will be put in museums for people to see, and copies can be made and sold to the public.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. The United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team played in the nineteen eighty Winter Olympics from February thirteenth to twenty fourth, nineteen eighty, in Lake Placid, New York. Team USA was made up of college players who defeated the defending Olympic champion Soviet Union team four to three on February twenty second, nineteen eighty. The nineteen eighty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team had twenty players including Bill Baker, Neal Broten, Dave Christian, Steve Christoff, Jim Craig, Mike Eruzione, John Harrington, Steve Janaszak, Mark Johnson, Rob McClanahan, Ken Morrow, Jack O'Callahan, Mark Pavelich, Mike Ramsey, Buzz Schneider, Dave Silk, Eric Strobel, Bob Suter, Mark Wells, and Phil Verchota. The game between the United States and Soviet Union was called the Miracle on Ice and was shown on television on February twenty second, nineteen eighty. It drew thirty four million two hundred thousand viewers on average. The match is remembered as a miracle because college hockey players beat a Soviet team that had won four gold medals in a row since nineteen sixty four. Team USA then defeated Finland four to one in their final game to win the gold medal, their first gold medal since nineteen sixty in men's ice hockey. Herb Brooks coached the nineteen eighty team. He was the last player cut from the nineteen sixty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team that won gold. Brooks was known as someone who motivated players and built a team around hard work and believing in yourself and your teammates. This tournament happened when the United States was dealing with economic problems, high gas prices, American hostages held in Iran, and tensions with the Soviet Union. The Soviet Union had invaded Afghanistan which led to the United States boycotting the nineteen eighty Summer Olympics. The Miracle on Ice was a turning point for ice hockey in the United States. Sports Illustrated named the game the greatest sports moment of the twentieth century. This historic win brought ice hockey to the front page of newspapers everywhere and opened the door to the National Hockey League for players born in the United States. The impact of this event is still felt today. Since nineteen eighty, interest in ice hockey in the United States has grown greatly. Registrations with USA Hockey have increased by nearly four hundred percent since nineteen eighty from one hundred thirty six thousand to over five hundred sixty four thousand. The number of National Hockey League players from the United States has increased from seventy two in nineteen eighty to two hundred forty five in twenty twenty four.

sections.title: Section 3  Congressional Gold Medals
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore (the temporary president) of the Senate will arrange for Congress to award three gold medals with the right design to the members of the nineteen eighty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team. This award recognizes their extraordinary achievement at the thirteenth Olympic Winter Games where college players defeated the powerful Soviet hockey team in the historic Miracle on Ice. This victory lifted morale in the United States during the height of the Cold War (a period of tension between the US and Soviet Union), inspired generations, and changed the sport of ice hockey in the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury will make gold medals with suitable symbols, designs, and words to be decided by the Secretary. After the gold medals are awarded, one gold medal will be given to the Lake Placid Olympic Center in Lake Placid, New York, where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate. One gold medal will be given to the United States Hockey Hall of Fame Museum in Eveleth, Minnesota, where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate. One gold medal will be given to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum in Colorado Springs, Colorado, where it will be displayed and made available for research when appropriate.

sections.title: Section 4  Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell bronze copies of the gold medals made under section three. The price must be enough to cover the costs including labor, materials, dies (tools used to make medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5  Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this Act are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one, United States Code (a section of federal law). For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one, United States Code, all medals made under this Act will be considered to be numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6  Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund can be charged for the amounts needed to pay for the costs of the medals made under this Act. Money received from the sale of duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four will be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
